cards.definition: the framework of bones in the body — functions: support, protection, movement, blood cell production, mineral storage
where two or more bones meet — allows movement. Types include hinge, ball and socket, pivot, condyloid
smooth tissue covering the ends of bones at joints — reduces friction and absorbs shock
a tough band of fibrous tissue connecting bone to bone — stabilises joints
a tough cord of fibrous tissue connecting muscle to bone — transmits the force of muscle contraction
muscles generate force by contracting — agonist (prime mover) contracts; antagonist relaxes
the muscle primarily responsible for a movement — also called the prime mover
the muscle that relaxes and lengthens as the agonist contracts — works in pairs
muscle produces force but does not change length — e.g. holding a position
muscle changes length — concentric (shortens) or eccentric (lengthens under tension)

questions.question: What is the function of ligaments?
options: A) Connect muscle to bone
B) Connect bone to bone — stabilising joints
C) Reduce friction at joints
D) Transmit nerve signals
questions.explanation: Ligaments are tough fibrous tissues connecting bone to bone — they stabilise joints and prevent excessive movement.

questions.question: What is an agonist muscle?
options: A) A muscle that relaxes during movement
B) The primary muscle responsible for a movement
C) A muscle that opposes the prime mover
D) A muscle that holds a position statically
questions.explanation: The agonist (prime mover) contracts to produce movement. During a bicep curl, the bicep is the agonist.

questions.question: What is isotonic muscle contraction?
options: A) Muscle produces force without changing length
B) Muscle changes length — concentric (shortens) or eccentric (lengthens)
C) Muscle contraction at maximum strength
D) Contraction only during isometric exercises
questions.explanation: Isotonic contractions involve length changes: concentric (muscle shortens during contraction) and eccentric (muscle lengthens under tension).

questions.question: What is cartilage?
options: A) Connects muscles to bones
B) Connects bones to bones
C) Smooth tissue covering bone ends at joints — reduces friction
D) A type of muscle tissue
questions.explanation: Articular cartilage covers the ends of bones at synovial joints — it reduces friction and absorbs impact during movement.

questions.question: What is a tendon?
options: A) Connects bone to bone
B) Reduces friction at joints
C) Connects muscle to bone — transmits force
D) Provides joint stability
questions.explanation: Tendons are strong fibrous cords connecting muscle to bone — they transmit the pulling force of muscle contractions.

questions.question: What is the antagonist muscle?
options: A) The primary mover during a movement
B) The muscle that relaxes and lengthens as the agonist contracts
C) A muscle holding a joint stable
D) The muscle generating the most force
questions.explanation: Muscles work in pairs: as the agonist contracts, the antagonist relaxes. During a bicep curl, the bicep is the agonist; the tricep is the antagonist.

questions.question: What is isometric contraction?
options: A) Muscle shortens during contraction
B) Muscle lengthens during contraction
C) Muscle produces force without changing length
D) Rapid muscle contraction for power
questions.explanation: Isometric contractions occur when a muscle generates force but neither shortens nor lengthens — e.g. holding a plank position.

questions.question: What type of joint is the knee?
options: A) Ball and socket
B) Pivot
C) Hinge
D) Condyloid
questions.explanation: The knee is a hinge joint — it allows movement in one plane (flexion and extension). The hip is a ball and socket joint.

questions.question: What is the function of cartilage at a joint?
options: A) Connect bones together
B) Connect muscles to bones
C) Reduce friction and absorb shock at bone ends
D) Transmit nerve impulses
questions.explanation: Articular cartilage covers the bone ends in synovial joints — its smooth surface reduces friction and its compressible structure absorbs shock.

questions.question: What is the role of the skeletal system in blood cell production?
options: A) Bones store calcium
B) Red and white blood cells are produced in red bone marrow
C) Bones transmit nerve signals
D) Bones store glycogen
questions.explanation: Red bone marrow (found in spongy bone) produces red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets — an essential function of the skeletal system.

questions.question: Ligaments connect muscle to bone.
questions.explanation: Ligaments connect BONE TO BONE. Tendons connect muscle to bone.

questions.question: The agonist muscle contracts during a movement.
questions.explanation: The agonist is the prime mover — it contracts to produce the movement.

questions.question: Isometric contractions involve the muscle changing length.
questions.explanation: In isometric contractions, the muscle generates force but does NOT change length.

questions.question: Cartilage reduces friction at joints.
questions.explanation: Articular cartilage is a smooth tissue covering bone ends — it reduces friction and absorbs shock.

questions.question: The antagonist muscle contracts at the same time as the agonist.
questions.explanation: The antagonist RELAXES as the agonist contracts — they work in opposing pairs.

questions.question: The knee is a ball and socket joint.
questions.explanation: The knee is a hinge joint. The hip and shoulder are ball and socket joints.

questions.question: Tendons connect bone to bone.
questions.explanation: Tendons connect MUSCLE TO BONE. Ligaments connect bone to bone.

questions.question: Red blood cells are produced in red bone marrow.
questions.explanation: Red bone marrow in spongy bone produces red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.

questions.question: Concentric contraction occurs when a muscle shortens.
questions.explanation: Concentric contraction: muscle shortens under tension — e.g. the bicep shortening during a curl.

questions.question: An eccentric contraction involves a muscle lengthening under tension.
questions.explanation: Eccentric contraction: muscle lengthens while under load — e.g. the bicep lengthening as you lower a weight.

questions.question: ________ connect bone to bone — stabilising joints.
questions.explanation: Torn ligaments are a common sports injury — especially at the knee and ankle.

questions.question: ________ connect muscle to bone — transmitting force.
questions.explanation: The Achilles tendon connects the calf muscle to the heel bone.

questions.question: ________ covers bone ends at joints — reducing friction and absorbing shock.
questions.explanation: Damaged cartilage can lead to joint pain and arthritis.

questions.question: The ________ is the prime mover — the muscle primarily responsible for a movement.
questions.explanation: During a squat, the quadriceps are the agonist (prime mover).

questions.question: The ________ muscle relaxes as the agonist contracts.
questions.explanation: Agonist and antagonist work in pairs — bicep/tricep is a classic example.

questions.question: ________ contraction: muscle generates force without changing length.
questions.explanation: Holding a plank or a wall sit involves isometric contraction.

questions.question: ________ contraction: muscle changes length — concentric (shortens) or eccentric (lengthens).
questions.explanation: Most everyday movements involve isotonic contractions.

questions.question: The ________ joint allows movement in one plane only — e.g. the knee and elbow.
questions.explanation: Hinge joints allow flexion and extension only.

questions.question: The ________ system provides support, protection, movement, and blood cell production.
questions.explanation: The 206 bones of the adult skeleton perform these vital functions.

questions.question: A ________ and socket joint allows movement in all planes — e.g. hip and shoulder.
questions.explanation: Ball and socket joints have the greatest range of movement of any joint type.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: connects bone to bone — stabilises joints
connects muscle to bone — transmits force
prime mover — contracts to produce movement
relaxes as agonist contracts — opposing pair
force produced — no change in muscle length

cards.definition: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — variables to manipulate when designing a training programme
gradually increasing the demands of training to continue improving — the body adapts to greater stress
training should be specific to the sport and the muscles/energy systems used
fitness gains are lost if training stops — 'use it or lose it'
training should be tailored to the individual's needs, ability, and goals
the maximum amount of oxygen the body can use during intense exercise — a measure of cardiovascular fitness
alternating periods of high-intensity work and rest — improves both aerobic and anaerobic fitness
a series of different exercises performed at stations — develops all-round fitness
explosive jumping exercises that improve muscular power — e.g. box jumps, depth jumps
Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — first aid treatment for soft tissue injuries

questions.question: What does FITT stand for?
options: A) Flexibility, Intensity, Time, Training
B) Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type
C) Fitness, Intervals, Training, Technique
D) Force, Intensity, Training, Time
questions.explanation: FITT principle: Frequency (how often), Intensity (how hard), Time (how long), Type (what kind of training).

questions.question: What is progressive overload?
options: A) Training as hard as possible from day one
B) Reducing training load over time
C) Gradually increasing training demands so the body continues to adapt
D) Performing the same training every session
questions.explanation: Progressive overload ensures the body is continually challenged — adaptations occur only when training exceeds what the body is used to.

questions.question: What is specificity in training?
options: A) Training as many muscles as possible
B) Training designed for the specific demands of a sport and its muscles/energy systems
C) Avoiding overtraining
D) Training at the same intensity every session
questions.explanation: Specificity: a swimmer should train their swimming muscles and energy systems — not just general fitness.

questions.question: What is reversibility?
options: A) Fitness gains are permanent
B) Fitness levels can be improved by reversing training methods
C) Fitness gains are lost if training stops
D) Training too hard causes injury
questions.explanation: Reversibility ('use it or lose it'): without training, adaptations gained reverse — fitness decreases relatively quickly.

questions.question: What is VO₂ max?
options: A) Maximum weight lifted in training
B) Maximum heart rate during exercise
C) Maximum oxygen the body can use during intense exercise
D) Volume of air breathed per minute
questions.explanation: VO₂ max is the gold standard measure of cardiovascular fitness — elite endurance athletes have much higher VO₂ max than untrained individuals.

questions.question: What is interval training?
options: A) Training at a single steady pace
B) Alternating periods of high-intensity work and rest
C) Training at maximum intensity only
D) A circuit of different exercises
questions.explanation: Interval training improves both aerobic and anaerobic fitness — the high-intensity periods stress the anaerobic system; recovery periods stress aerobic recovery.

questions.question: What is plyometrics?
options: A) Stretching exercises for flexibility
B) Endurance running training
C) Explosive jumping exercises improving muscular power
D) Weight training for strength
questions.explanation: Plyometrics uses the stretch-shortening cycle — rapid eccentric loading followed by explosive concentric contraction — to develop power.

questions.question: What is RICE treatment?
options: A) A type of training method
B) Rest, Intensity, Compression, Elevation
C) Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — first aid for soft tissue injuries
D) A warm-up method
questions.explanation: RICE: Rest (stop activity), Ice (reduce swelling), Compression (support), Elevation (reduce blood flow) — immediate treatment for sprains and strains.

questions.question: What is circuit training?
options: A) Running in circuits
B) A series of different exercises at stations improving all-round fitness
C) Interval training on a track
D) Resistance training for one muscle group
questions.explanation: Circuit training involves moving between stations of different exercises — targeting different muscle groups and fitness components in one session.

questions.question: What does individual differences mean in training?
options: A) All athletes should follow the same programme
B) Training must be tailored to the individual's needs, ability, and goals
C) Only professional athletes need individual programmes
D) Training differences between male and female athletes only
questions.explanation: Individual differences: a beginner needs a different programme from an elite athlete — age, fitness level, goals, and health status all influence training design.

questions.question: FITT stands for Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type.
questions.explanation: These four variables are manipulated when designing and progressing a training programme.

questions.question: Progressive overload means training at the same level every session.
questions.explanation: Progressive overload means GRADUALLY INCREASING the demands — the body must be challenged beyond its current capacity to continue adapting.

questions.question: Specificity means training should match the demands of the sport.
questions.explanation: A sprinter should train differently from a marathon runner — different muscles and energy systems are required.

questions.question: Reversibility means fitness gains are permanent.
questions.explanation: Reversibility means fitness gains ARE LOST if training stops — 'use it or lose it'.

questions.question: VO₂ max is a measure of muscular strength.
questions.explanation: VO₂ max measures CARDIOVASCULAR fitness — the maximum oxygen the body can use during intense exercise.

questions.question: Interval training alternates high-intensity work with rest periods.
questions.explanation: Interval training improves both aerobic and anaerobic fitness through work-rest cycles.

questions.question: Plyometrics develops cardiovascular endurance.
questions.explanation: Plyometrics develops MUSCULAR POWER through explosive jumping and stretch-shortening cycle exercises.

questions.question: RICE treatment includes Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation.
questions.explanation: RICE is the standard first aid for soft tissue injuries such as sprains and strains.

questions.question: Circuit training focuses on one muscle group at a time.
questions.explanation: Circuit training targets multiple fitness components and muscle groups through a variety of exercises at different stations.

questions.question: Individual differences mean all athletes should follow identical training programmes.
questions.explanation: Training must be tailored to the individual's needs, fitness level, age, and goals.

questions.question: FITT stands for Frequency, Intensity, Time, and ________.
questions.explanation: These four variables are adjusted when designing and progressing a training programme.

questions.question: ________ overload: gradually increasing training demands so the body continues to adapt.
questions.explanation: Without overload, the body has no reason to adapt — fitness plateaus.

questions.question: ________ means training should match the specific demands of the sport.
questions.explanation: A cyclist should train the muscles and energy systems used in cycling.

questions.question: ________ means fitness gains are lost if training stops — 'use it or lose it'.
questions.explanation: Cardiovascular fitness decreases relatively quickly when training stops.

questions.question: ________ max is the maximum oxygen the body can use — a measure of cardiovascular fitness.
questions.explanation: Elite endurance athletes have VO₂ max values much higher than untrained individuals.

questions.question: ________ training alternates high-intensity work with rest or recovery periods.
questions.explanation: Interval training develops both aerobic and anaerobic fitness.

questions.question: ________ are explosive jumping exercises developing muscular power.
questions.explanation: Plyometrics uses the stretch-shortening cycle to develop explosive power.

questions.question: RICE: Rest, Ice, ________, Elevation — first aid for soft tissue injuries.
questions.explanation: Compression reduces swelling; elevation reduces blood flow to the injured area.

questions.question: ________ training uses a series of exercise stations to develop all-round fitness.
questions.explanation: Circuit training targets different muscle groups and fitness components in one session.

questions.question: ________ differences mean training must be tailored to the individual's needs and goals.
questions.explanation: Age, fitness level, health status, and goals all influence appropriate training.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: gradually increase demands — body continues to adapt
training matches demands of the sport
fitness lost if training stops — use it or lose it
max oxygen use — gold standard of cardiovascular fitness

cards.definition: the drive to achieve a goal — intrinsic (from within) or extrinsic (from external rewards)
motivation driven by internal satisfaction — enjoyment, personal achievement, mastery
motivation driven by external rewards — trophies, money, praise
deliberately harming another person physically or psychologically — outside the rules
forceful, competitive play within the rules — not the same as aggression
a state of mental and physical activation — affects performance
Yerkes-Dodson — performance improves with arousal up to an optimum, then declines if over-aroused
SMART goals — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound — improves motivation
regular exercise reduces anxiety and depression — improves mood, self-esteem, and sleep
performance improves in the presence of an audience — for well-learned skills; declines for new skills

questions.question: What is intrinsic motivation?
options: A) Motivation from trophies and money
B) Motivation from praise from a coach
C) Internal motivation from enjoyment and personal satisfaction
D) Motivation from competitive goals
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within — playing for the love of the sport, personal challenge, and the satisfaction of improvement.

questions.question: What is the inverted U theory?
options: A) Performance decreases as arousal increases
B) Performance increases with any amount of arousal
C) Performance improves with increasing arousal up to an optimum, then declines
D) Arousal has no effect on performance
questions.explanation: Yerkes-Dodson: too little arousal = under-performance; optimal arousal = best performance; too much arousal = anxiety and declining performance.

questions.question: What is the difference between aggression and assertion?
options: A) Both are the same
B) Assertion involves harm; aggression is within the rules
C) Aggression deliberately harms; assertion is forceful but within the rules
D) Assertion is only verbal; aggression is physical
questions.explanation: Aggression deliberately harms — it is outside the rules. Assertion is competitive, forceful play within the rules — e.g. a hard but fair tackle.

questions.question: What are SMART goals?
options: A) Simple, Measurable, Accurate, Realistic, Timely
B) Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound
C) Strategic, Motivating, Achievable, Rewarding, Timely
D) Set, Manage, Assess, Record, Track
questions.explanation: SMART goal setting: Specific (clear), Measurable (trackable), Achievable (possible), Realistic (appropriate), Time-bound (deadline).

questions.question: What is social facilitation?
options: A) Playing better alone
B) Performance improving in the presence of an audience — for well-learned skills
C) Working cooperatively in a team
D) Improving through social media
questions.explanation: Zajonc's social facilitation: an audience improves performance of dominant (well-learned) responses but impairs performance of non-dominant (novel) skills.

questions.question: What mental health benefits does exercise provide?
options: A) Increases anxiety in the short term
B) Only benefits physical health
C) Reduces anxiety and depression — improves mood, self-esteem, and sleep
D) Mental health benefits only occur in elite athletes
questions.explanation: Regular exercise releases endorphins, reduces stress hormones, improves sleep quality, and boosts self-esteem — well-evidenced mental health benefits.

questions.question: What is extrinsic motivation?
options: A) Internal satisfaction from sport
B) Motivation from enjoyment and mastery
C) Motivation from external rewards — trophies, money, or praise
D) Motivation to beat personal records
questions.explanation: Extrinsic motivation comes from outside — medals, prizes, praise, or recognition. Over-reliance on extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation.

questions.question: What happens to performance if arousal is too high according to inverted U theory?
options: A) Performance continues to improve
B) Performance stays the same
C) Performance declines — anxiety and over-arousal impair execution
D) Performance improves then stays stable
questions.explanation: Above the optimal arousal level, performance declines — muscles tighten, decision-making worsens, and anxiety disrupts skill execution.

questions.question: What is goal setting used for in sport?
options: A) To measure fitness only
B) To improve motivation and focus — SMART goals direct effort
C) To select team members
D) To measure arousal levels
questions.explanation: Goal setting directs effort, increases persistence, and improves motivation — SMART goals make progress measurable and achievable.

questions.question: What is arousal in the context of sport?
options: A) The level of physical fitness
B) A state of mental and physical activation affecting performance
C) A measure of aggression
D) The level of social support
questions.explanation: Arousal is a state of physiological and psychological readiness — too little leads to poor performance; too much leads to anxiety. The optimal level varies by sport and individual.

questions.question: Intrinsic motivation comes from external rewards.
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from WITHIN — enjoyment, personal achievement, and the love of the activity.

questions.question: The inverted U theory states performance always improves with more arousal.
questions.explanation: The inverted U theory shows performance DECLINES above the optimal arousal level.

questions.question: Aggression and assertion are the same thing.
questions.explanation: Aggression involves deliberate harm outside the rules. Assertion is forceful but within the rules.

questions.question: SMART goals include being Specific and Measurable.
questions.explanation: SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound.

questions.question: Social facilitation always improves performance.
questions.explanation: Social facilitation improves performance of WELL-LEARNED skills but impairs performance of NEW or COMPLEX skills.

questions.question: Regular exercise has proven mental health benefits.
questions.explanation: Exercise reduces anxiety and depression, improves mood, boosts self-esteem, and enhances sleep quality.

questions.question: Extrinsic motivation involves internal satisfaction.
questions.explanation: Extrinsic motivation comes from EXTERNAL sources — trophies, money, praise.

questions.question: Optimal arousal level is the same for all sports.
questions.explanation: Optimal arousal varies — contact sports require higher arousal; precision sports (archery) require lower arousal.

questions.question: Over-reliance on extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation.
questions.explanation: When external rewards are removed, motivation may drop below original levels — this is the overjustification effect.

questions.question: Goal setting using SMART criteria improves motivation and performance.
questions.explanation: SMART goals provide clear targets, measurable progress, and appropriate challenge — all of which improve motivation.

questions.question: ________ motivation comes from within — enjoyment and personal satisfaction.
questions.explanation: Intrinsically motivated athletes play for the love of the sport, not for external rewards.

questions.question: ________ motivation comes from external rewards — trophies, money, praise.
questions.explanation: Extrinsic rewards can be effective short-term but may undermine intrinsic motivation if overused.

questions.question: ________ deliberately harms another person and is outside the rules.
questions.explanation: Aggression in sport violates the rules and may result in penalties or disciplinary action.

questions.question: ________ is forceful, competitive play within the rules — not the same as aggression.
questions.explanation: A hard but fair tackle is assertion, not aggression.

questions.question: The inverted ________ theory shows performance peaks at optimal arousal then declines.
questions.explanation: Yerkes-Dodson: too little or too much arousal both lead to poor performance.

questions.question: SMART stands for Specific, Measurable, ________, Realistic, Time-bound.
questions.explanation: SMART goals make progress trackable and targets appropriately challenging.

questions.question: Social ________ improves performance of well-learned skills in front of an audience.
questions.explanation: Zajonc: audiences enhance dominant responses but impair non-dominant (new) skills.

questions.question: Regular exercise reduces ________ and depression — proven mental health benefits.
questions.explanation: Exercise releases endorphins and reduces cortisol — improving mood and mental wellbeing.

questions.question: ________ is a state of mental and physical activation that affects performance.
questions.explanation: Optimal arousal varies by sport — too little or too much impairs performance.

questions.question: SMART ________ setting directs effort and increases motivation and persistence.
questions.explanation: Clear, achievable goals with deadlines give athletes focus and measurable progress.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: performance peaks at optimal arousal — declines above it
